bootstrap.author: read_only_friday
bootstrap.title: Our deploy freeze has a name, a laminated sign, and an origin story
bootstrap.body:
The rule: no deploys after Thursday lunch. The sign: laminated, above my desk. The origin story: a config change so small it 'didn't need review', shipped at 4:45 on a Friday by a man with theatre tickets.

The change flipped a cache setting. The cache then faithfully served everyone the same shopping cart. One cart, many customers, one entire weekend of my life.

He's a director now. The sign remains. Ask me anything.

comments.author: packet_loss
comments.body: be honest with the group. somewhere underneath that cache setting, was it DNS

replies.author: read_only_friday
replies.body: It was a stale service lookup behind the cache layer, which is DNS wearing a suit. I hate that you knew.

comments.author: CtrlAltDelight
comments.body: QA translation of 'so small it didn't need review': the most dangerous sentence in software. Big changes get respect. Small changes get weekends.

comments.author: APIdontknow
comments.body: I deployed on a Friday once. the word once is doing a lot of work in this sentence

comments.author: airgapped_alice
comments.body: one cart, many customers is simultaneously an incident report, a philosophy lecture, and a country song

bootstrap.author: APIdontknow
bootstrap.title: The bug vanished when I added a log line. The log line ships to prod tonight
bootstrap.body:
Race condition, probably. Can't reproduce it with the log line in, can't catch it with the log line out, and so I have written my first load-bearing print statement.

The comment above it says DO NOT REMOVE, and I mean it as both documentation and prayer.

comments.author: CtrlAltDelight
comments.body: congratulations on your first heisenbug. the log line changes the timing just enough to hide the race. it's not fixed, it's wearing a disguise, and one day the disguise falls off during a demo

replies.author: APIdontknow
replies.body: 'not fixed, wearing a disguise' is going in the ticket. the ticket I am absolutely opening and never closing

comments.author: read_only_friday
comments.body: We have a sleep for 100 milliseconds from 2016 with the comment 'ask Dave'. Dave left in 2019. The sleep stays. It has seniority now.

comments.author: packet_loss
comments.body: somewhere out there is a network closet where a switch only works because of the vibration from its own cooling fan. your log line is fine. your log line is family

comments.author: null_terminated
comments.body: The senior move is making the load-bearing log line at least log something useful, so in two years the archaeology team gets a clue along with their curse.

bootstrap.author: CtrlAltDelight
bootstrap.title: Bug titles I have actually filed: a retrospective from ten years of QA
bootstrap.body:
A selection from the greatest hits folder:

'User can order negative three sandwiches; refund system agrees enthusiastically.'

'Search returns results before you finish typing, but only wrong ones.'

'Deleting your account promotes you to admin.'

'Calendar accepts February 30th if you scroll fast enough.'

Every single one of these reached staging wrapped in full confidence. Stay humble, write weird tests.

comments.author: APIdontknow
comments.body: deleting your account promotes you to admin is the greatest speedrun strat ever published

replies.author: CtrlAltDelight
replies.body: the dev's first response was, verbatim, 'well don't do that.' we had it framed.

comments.author: null_terminated
comments.body: 'Well don't do that' is the first stage of grief. I know all five personally. The last one is a regression test.

comments.author: read_only_friday
comments.body: Negative three sandwiches would absolutely have paged me at 3 a.m. as a billing discrepancy. Thank you for your service.

comments.author: airgapped_alice
comments.body: the february 30th one haunts me, not because I know which date library it was, but because it could be all of them

bootstrap.author: packet_loss
bootstrap.title: It's never DNS. It's always DNS. Both statements are load-bearing and I hold them simultaneously
bootstrap.body:
Last month's masterpiece: intermittent failures, one office only. Over two days we blamed the firewall, the VPN, one specific switch, one specific cable, and briefly, cosmic rays. There were packet captures. There was a whiteboard.

It was a DNS record someone had updated the week before — with a time-to-live of 24 hours. Half the machines were still holding the old answer, half had the new one, and the overlap window turned the office into a coin flip. The network was innocent. The network is never innocent, except when it's DNS, which is always.

The haiku stays taped to my monitor: it's not DNS. there's no way it's DNS. it was DNS.

comments.author: greybeard_junior
comments.body: sysadmin solidarity. my version of the haiku is 'it's not the certificate' and you already know exactly how that story ends

replies.author: packet_loss
replies.body: it expired on a saturday, didn't it. it always expires on a saturday

comments.author: airgapped_alice
comments.body: DNS is the phone book of the internet, in the sense that it's ancient, everything depends on it, and nobody has verified it since 2014

comments.author: CtrlAltDelight
comments.body: half the machines holding one answer and half the other is my favourite genre of bug: every component worked exactly as designed, and that's the problem

drip.author: airgapped_alice
drip.title: Locked myself out of my password manager and briefly achieved perfect security
drip.body:
Changed my master passphrase Sunday night. Very proud, very sleepy. Monday morning, the muscle memory typed the old one. Then the new one, but wrong. Then something that may have been a grocery list.

For twenty minutes I was the most secure person on the planet. No attacker on earth could access my accounts. Neither could I. Zero trust: achieved, including in myself.

The emergency kit in the fire safe worked exactly as designed, which is the least funny possible ending, so I lead with the grocery list part.

comments.author: CtrlAltDelight
comments.body: sleepy sunday credential changes are QA catnip. you executed the classic test case: what if the user is also the attacker, but incompetent

comments.author: packet_loss
comments.body: you had a break-glass procedure AND it worked on the first try. in this economy. standing ovation from the entire ops section

replies.author: airgapped_alice
replies.body: my password manager having a password manager finally paid off. turns out the bit was preparation all along

comments.author: APIdontknow
comments.body: 'something that may have been a grocery list' is exactly how my brain stores every password older than a month

drip.author: null_terminated
drip.title: Code review comment translation guide, from someone who has written every single one of these
drip.body:
'nit:' — I will die on this hill, but quietly.

'Curious why we went this direction?' — I have already composed the eulogy for this approach.

'Can we add a test for this?' — I do not believe you, professionally.

'This is fine for now.' — I am marking the exact coordinates of the future incident, for the archaeologists.

'LGTM!' — I have reviewed the description, the diff stats, and the vibes.

comments.author: APIdontknow
comments.body: 'curious why we went this direction' made me exhale through my nose because I received it yesterday and yes, the direction is deceased

comments.author: CtrlAltDelight
comments.body: you forgot 'thoughts?' — which means I have located the fatal flaw and would like to watch you find it too

replies.author: null_terminated
replies.body: 'thoughts?' is Socratic cruelty and I respect it enormously.

comments.author: read_only_friday
comments.body: 'this is fine for now' has paged me more than any other four words in the English language. 'for now' should legally require an expiry date.

drip.author: CtrlAltDelight
drip.title: 'Works on my machine' is a hypothesis, not a verdict
drip.body:
Dev: works on my machine. Me: wonderful news, we'll ship your machine. The joke is now old enough that the punchline became an industry — we genuinely do ship the machine these days, we just call it a container image.

My actual favourite moment of the job is when 'works on my machine' meets 'here is a video of it not working on mine.' The five stages of grief get faster every year. The acceptance stage is usually an environment variable.

comments.author: packet_loss
comments.body: the acceptance stage is usually an environment variable, or as we say in networking, an MTU setting

comments.author: read_only_friday
comments.body: we containerized everything and the sentence simply evolved: works in my container. the machine was never the problem. the machine was us all along

comments.author: airgapped_alice
comments.body: a video of the bug is the QA equivalent of a notarized affidavit, and I would bet money devs still ask if you cleared your cache

replies.author: CtrlAltDelight
replies.body: they ask. I clear it on camera now. we have developed theatre for this.

drip.author: APIdontknow
drip.title: Told my lead the ticket would take two days. Day four update: it will take two days
drip.body:
The two days are load-bearing. They have always been two days. Time moves; the estimate does not. I understand senior devs now — they don't estimate better, they just say 'it depends' with the confidence of someone who has been wrong more times than me.

Anyway, the actual blocker: fixing the bug revealed a second, older bug, which the first bug had been accidentally fixing. I am now the maintainer of a small bug ecosystem.

comments.author: CtrlAltDelight
comments.body: two bugs cancelling each other out is called a stable system. you didn't hit a blocker, you performed an ecological intervention

comments.author: read_only_friday
comments.body: estimates given in days remain in days. that's the rule. the number stays fixed and the unit quietly becomes a metaphor.

comments.author: packet_loss
comments.body: the first bug was load-bearing too. it's load-bearing all the way down, kid. welcome to production

late_comments.author: null_terminated
late_comments.body: The director promotion is the most realistic detail in this post. The incident was the interview.

late_comments.author: airgapped_alice
late_comments.body: update from this thread radicalizing me: went and labelled our load-bearing print statements. we have nine. one is in a language we no longer use anywhere else

late_comments.author: packet_loss
late_comments.body: 'deleting your account promotes you to admin' is why QA people are the only ones I trust anywhere near my firewall configs

late_comments.author: read_only_friday
late_comments.body: arriving late to point out the real lesson: if you know a record change is coming, drop the TTL to something short a day ahead, make the change, then raise it back. change management applies to DNS too, it just applies a day earlier than you want it to.
